episode_title: Upasna Gautam: The Product Manager's Guide to AI in News

codes.description: Argument that a strong, flexible CMS platform foundation must exist before any AI capabilities can be layered on top
excerpts: AI isn't going to take you ever from zero to one. You have to have a strong foundation to build upon. And Stellar has been that. You know, we spent years building, optimizing, and migrating onto Stellar.
it allows you to adopt and adapt as user needs change
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: CMS serving two distinct user groups: editorial teams (content creation/publishing) and product/engineering teams (building/testing product experiences)
excerpts: it supports two key stakeholder sets. One of them is of course enabling our editorial teams to create, edit, and publish content and program different content experiences across CNN... And then our second stakeholder set, which is an interesting one and we don't really see a lot in news, is Stellar enables our product and engineering teams to build, test, and learn faster
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Identifying automation of mundane, repetitive tasks (tagging, transcription, translation) as the highest-impact AI opportunity
excerpts: the one area that has the most immense promise is workflow orchestration and optimization. This is automating mundane tasks, repetitive tasks, so journalists can focus on creativity, strategy, reporting. So, you know, AI powered tools can streamline basic things like content tagging or transcription or translation
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Surfacing audience performance data and actionable insights directly within the editing interface in real time
excerpts: How can we integrate data directly into the editorial workflow? How can we surface data and important information for our editors? As they are in the editing experience in the platform. So that could look like something like a dashboard that shows how a story is performing, but also provides actionable insights such as suggesting better headlines or identifying high-performing story formats in real time.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Reusing modular content assets (videos, text blocks, images) across platforms to scale content creation
excerpts: the third area that holds the most promise for improving editorial efficiency through AI is content modularity. And this is like how do we reuse assets like videos, text blocks, images to help scale content creation across platforms.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.code: Speed and reliability as north star
codes.description: Using speed and reliability as the two core metrics guiding all platform decisions and AI prioritization
excerpts: my work involves orchestrating a complex ecosystem of tools, workflows, and components that need to meet this dual demand of speed and reliability, which are my two key metrics of success.
at the world's biggest breaking news organization, it's everything, right? but also balancing integrity with speed, accuracy with speed, reliability with speed.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Warning against deploying AI for the sake of innovation rather than solving clearly defined problems
excerpts: Not every inefficiency warrants an AI solution. And this is not just AI related. I think this is pro AI. especially now because it's shiny object syndrome around it. But in general, when it comes to product development, we tend to skip over the product discovery process and immediately start making assumptions to create a solution.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Structured approach to AI adoption: understand user pain points, evaluate AI strengths and trade-offs, align with key success metrics
excerpts: I begin by Talking to my users in depth, observing workflows, conducting interviews, identifying the bottlenecks or the blockers, Is this a problem about speed? Is it a problem about scalability? Is it a problem about accuracy? What problem are we trying to solve? And why is that problem a problem in the first place?
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Industry-wide pattern of rushing to implement AI without understanding the underlying problem being addressed
excerpts: There is this sometimes blind race to just adopt without understanding the problem that's actually being solved.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Strategic framing of AI to editorial teams as friction-eliminator rather than job threat
excerpts: I always position Once I have a very clear POV on what I want to solve a problem for, is I position AI as an enabler and not a replacement. So how can you demonstrate how AI can eliminate that friction, the bottlenecks, and free them to focus on high-value work?
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Building editorial-product collaboration through long-term relationship investment, transparency, and empathy before introducing AI
excerpts: that collaboration starts with building trust through transparency and empathy. And that starts way before conversations about AI.
having really, really strong relationships with my editorial partners and stakeholders because I've taken them along with me for the ride to co-create and co-develop.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Involving editorial staff early in AI product development as sponsors, advocates, and teammates rather than passive recipients
excerpts: Involving editorial teams early in the process, whether they're your customer or not, and co-designing solutions and gathering feedback with them. It's like the ultimate product management hack in news. Like they need to be your sponsors, your advocates, your teammates.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Foundational discovery framework: What's your biggest goal? What's your biggest pain point? How do you measure success?
excerpts: What's your biggest goal for you and your team? What's your biggest pain point? And three, how do you measure success? Because your job can be and should be to help them achieve those goals and hit those key metrics of success.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Insistence on making AI's value proposition measurable and communicable to editorial stakeholders
excerpts: that's why I think having those key metrics of success is so important because otherwise you cannot quantify or qualify the value of this thing you're doing, right?
How can you quantify and qualify the trade-off? So it's very clear to the end user that by adopting this This is the benefit you are going to receive.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Strategy of piloting AI tools with small, enthusiastic teams before organization-wide scaling
excerpts: prototyping, piloting tools with small, willing teams, you know, before scaling it also builds confidence.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Embedding AI literacy into everyday communications rather than relying solely on formal training programs
excerpts: I also prioritize ongoing education and not in like a formal way. I mean infusing it into every Communication or mode of communication I have and explaining AI's limitations and capabilities so that they feel empowered. And not intimidated.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: CNN structuring product/technology teams like a tech company with squads, product leads, tech leads, and design leads
excerpts: It works very much like a tech company, and we're evolving more and more to adopt a lot of those similar organizational frameworks and structures.
each team has a product lead, a tech lead, and a design lead. I would say our current staff of product technology and design is about three fifty to four hundred people.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Core platform team functioning as internal consultants to product squads building on the shared platform
excerpts: The platform team, since we serve the internal customer, which is our journalist... we also function as consultants for those other squads because they're building features and products on our platform on Stellar. And so we work with them to validate requirements, to identify any gaps.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Presidential election coverage as validation of platform robustness, achieving zero critical incidents
excerpts: We just ran a presidential election on it with zero critical incidents. So from a product and technology standpoint, it was smooth and clean and nearly seamless.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Multi-year process of building a new platform from scratch, including a year of team-by-team onboarding and training
excerpts: It was truly taking a product from zero to one. Our legacy platform was legacy, so you can imagine the problems that it had.
there was a whole phase, almost a year of training and onboarding. And we started T and we went team by team by team.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Framing AI as amplifying existing capabilities rather than creating new ones -- good skills get better, bad skills get worse
excerpts: If you're a good writer or a good coder, it will make you an even better writer or an even better coder. But if you suck at writing and you're a bad coder, it will make you even worse.
It's a force multiplier and it helps you move faster and with more precision.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Argument that foundational product management work -- problem identification, vision-setting, strategic decisions -- remains irreducibly human
excerpts: the foundational work, the human work of identifying the right problems to solve, asking the right questions, setting the scale clear purpose-driven vision. Like you can't outsource that stuff to AI. And it's also if you do, it's very easily recognizable.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Personal use of ChatGPT for drafting documents, synthesizing information, exploring alternatives, and tailoring communication to diverse stakeholders
excerpts: I have chat GPT is always open in my browser tab. I love it. I use it all the time.

I use AI all the time, regularly, to draft initial versions of documents, to synthesize large amounts of information. I also sometimes use it to explore alternative approaches to a problem. to translate highly technical documentation into a explain it to me like I'm five
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Using AI to reframe messages for diverse global audiences and draft operational templates at scale
excerpts: because I work with hundreds of stakeholders, partners users across the globe. I use it to reframe communication and tailor messages for diverse audiences, draft templates for operational processes.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Personal journey from AI dismissal to daily dependence within a year
excerpts: last year at this time, I was just like whatever. This is just some weird trendy thing that's gonna bust. And after playing around with it, I very quickly saw that it amplifies the things that you're good at.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.code: Asking right questions as top challenge
codes.description: Identifying the inability to properly frame problems as the greatest industry-wide challenge with AI, ahead of technical challenges
excerpts: The greatest challenge is asking the right questions to solve the right problems. And this isn't just in journalism, it's all across tech and product. But especially it is a dire situation for journalism because of the eroding trust we've had in our industry over the past couple of years.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Core tension between AI accelerating content creation and its potential to amplify misinformation without safeguards
excerpts: AI can accelerate content creation and curation, but it also very clearly and very easily amplifies misinformation if it's not carefully managed. So how do we create robust safeguards, guardrails, and ethical frameworks to make sure that those AI-driven tools operate with journalistic integrity.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Gap between well-resourced and under-resourced newsrooms in ability to adopt AI due to resources, knowledge, and talent
excerpts: the inequality of access to a lot of these AI tools and the development of the technology. You know, there's clearly an industry-wide gap. Again, the under-resourced newsrooms cannot adopt as quickly because maybe they don't have the resour like they literally don't have the resources, but also the knowledge or the information to know how to do it.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Industry organization sharing knowledge freely to help under-resourced newsrooms access AI capabilities and product management practices
excerpts: why I love the news product alliance is that we get to develop the resources and share the knowledge, you know, freely, literally and figuratively freely, so that we can equip those smaller or under-resourced newsrooms with this information that lives, you know, in big tech or larger newsrooms so that, you know, we can all innovate at the speed of news.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Advice for smaller newsrooms to focus on one big problem broken into smaller pieces rather than trying to solve everything
excerpts: you cannot solve every problem at once. And especially the smaller the newsroom is. Focus on the one big problem and break that problem down into smaller pieces. What is that North Star problem?
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: News industry's inability to attract top technical talent compared to other industries
excerpts: we don't have the ability, like other industries still, to attract top tech talent for many reasons. That's a whole nother conversation.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Framing the CMS not as a finished product but as a cyclical, continuously iterating system that will always be evolving
excerpts: the product development. Process is cyclical. It's there's no finite beginning and end. The CMS is one of those products that is so robust that it will always be evolving.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Difficulty of consolidating dispersed audience data and team knowledge into centralized, accessible systems in large newsrooms
excerpts: You have this massive collection of highly valuable information and data about your audiences, but also about other teams. What are other teams working on? What are their content strategies? What are their goals? And all because a lot of it overlaps. And it's like, how can we help internally our editors collaborate more efficiently?
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

codes.description: Legitimate editorial concerns that AI might erode journalistic values, replace human judgment, or eliminate jobs
excerpts: Editorial teams, of course, and rightfully so, fear that AI might erode journalistic values or replace human judgment, take their jobs in a time where It's already really sensitive and difficult and challenging.
codes.speaker: Upasna Gautam

episode_summary: Upasna Gautam offers a distinctly product-management-centered perspective on AI in news, anchored in the principle that strong platform foundations must precede AI adoption -- crystallized in her repeated maxim that 'AI won't take you from zero to one.' Her most practically transferable contribution is a structured methodology for AI problem selection (understand pain points, evaluate trade-offs, align with success metrics) that explicitly counters the 'shiny object syndrome' she sees across the industry. The conversation reveals CNN operating with 350-400 product/technology/design staff organized in tech-company-style squads, a scale of investment that sharply illustrates the resource inequality she later identifies as the industry's third-biggest challenge. Notably, Gautam's own skeptic-to-convert arc with ChatGPT -- dismissing it a year prior, then using it daily as a force multiplier -- mirrors the editorial adoption journey she advocates managing through transparency, empathy, and quantified trade-offs.
surprises: CNN has 350-400 product, technology, and design staff -- a scale of investment that dwarfs most newsrooms and underscores the inequality Gautam herself names as a top industry challenge
Gautam admits she dismissed ChatGPT as 'some weird trendy thing that's gonna bust' just one year before the interview, an unusually candid skeptic-to-convert narrative from a senior PM
The Stellar platform migration included nearly a year dedicated solely to team-by-team onboarding and training, suggesting the human change management investment rivals the technical development
She frames the biggest industry challenge as 'asking the right questions' rather than any technical limitation, placing product thinking above engineering capability
AI as 'force multiplier' cuts both ways -- she explicitly says it makes bad writers worse, an uncommonly blunt counterpoint to the typical 'AI helps everyone' narrative
tensions: Tension between CNN's tech-company scale (350-400 staff) and Gautam's advocacy for under-resourced newsrooms through the News Product Alliance -- the practices she describes may not translate to organizations with fundamentally different resource bases
Tension between 'AI won't take you from zero to one' and the reality that many newsrooms lack the strong platform foundation she identifies as prerequisite, potentially widening the gap
Tension between positioning AI as 'enabler not replacement' and the acknowledged reality that editorial teams 'rightfully' fear job displacement

Tension between the critique of 'shiny object syndrome' and Gautam's own admission that she has ChatGPT 'always open' in her browser -- the line between enthusiastic adoption and shiny-object chasing is thin
Tension between speed as north star metric at a breaking news organization and the need for careful, measured AI adoption with 'robust safeguards and ethical frameworks'
